description:
Kaggle environment wrapper for OpenSpiel games.
For game implementation details see:
https://github.com/google-deepmind/open_spiel/tree/master/open_spiel/games
name: open_spiel_go

action.description: Action object MUST contain a field `submission`, and MAY contain arbitrary additional information.

actTimeout.description: Maximum runtime (seconds) to obtain an action from an agent.

episodeSteps.description: Maximum number of steps in the episode.

includeLegalActions.description: If true, include legalActions and legalActionStrings in observations. Defaults to false since these can be derived from serializedGameAndState.

initialActions.description: Actions applied to initial state before play begins to set up starting position.

loadPresetHands.description: Repeated poker only. Load preset hand chance actions from preset_hands.jsonl.

openSpielGameName.description: The short_name of the OpenSpiel game to load.

openSpielGameParameters.description: Game parameters for Open Spiel game.

openSpielGameString.description: The full game string including parameters.

presetHands.description: Repeated poker only. List of per-hand chance action sequences to use instead of random chance.

runTimeout.description: Maximum runtime (seconds) of an episode (not necessarily DONE).

seed.description: Integer currently only used for selecting starting position.

useImage.description: If true, indicates the observation is intended to be rendered as an image. Note that currently the agent harness is responsible for the actual rendering; no image is passed in the observation.

useOpenings.description: Whether to start from a position in an opening book.

currentPlayer.description: ID of player whose turn it is.

legalActionStrings.description: List of OpenSpiel legal action strings. Only included if includeLegalActions is true.

legalActions.description: List of OpenSpiel legal action integers. Only included if includeLegalActions is true.

openSpielGameName.description: Short name of the OpenSpiel game.

playerId.description: ID of the agent receiving this observation.

serializedGameAndState.description: Enables reconstructing the Game and State objects.

remainingOverageTime.description: Total remaining banked time (seconds) that can be used in excess of per-step actTimeouts -- agent is disqualified with TIMEOUT status when this drops below 0.

action.thoughts:
The current position is a 13x13 board where Black has played a very central, influence-oriented opening by occupying the Tengen (G7) after placing two 4-4 stones on the right side (K4, K10). White has two 4-4 stones on the left side (D4, D10). 

By playing Tengen, Black is inviting a large-scale moyo (framework) game. If White plays passively or just builds the left side (e.g., with D7), Black will likely follow up with K7, creating a massive, perfectly symmetric framework on the right side that works flawlessly with Tengen. Since Black has the extra stone in the center, a symmetrical moyo-building contest will favor Black.

To counter this, White needs to prevent Black from forming the ideal "box" on the right side. The most active and standard way to do this is to approach one of Black's 4-4 stones. 

An approach at **H3** (a small knight's approach to the K4 stone) is an excellent, flexible choice. 
1. It immediately breaks the symmetry and prevents Black from naturally sealing the bottom right corner and right side.
2. If Black responds by developing the right side (e.g., K7), White can smoothly extend to F3. The resulting formation (D4, F3, H3) creates a perfect, resilient base on the bottom side, securing solid territory while negating much of the influence Black's Tengen might have exerted in that sector.
3. If Black chooses to pincer (e.g., F3), White has room to jump out to H5 or even take the 3-3 point (L3), rendering Black's stones somewhat over-concentrated.

Additionally, "h3" respects the safe zone of coordinate formatting constraints while applying immediate, necessary pressure on Black's strategy.

```json
{
  "move": "h3"
}
```

White has responded with two corner stones (D10, D4) and two low stones on the bottom side (H3, E3). White's previous move at E3 is an attempt to stabilize the bottom side and undermine Black's F4 stone.

The move **f3** is a strong and solid response. It directly addresses the pressure from White's E3 stone by performing a hane (or more precisely, a side-by-side extension from F4). This move accomplishes several goals:
1. **Splitting:** It maintains the pressure between White's E3 and H3 stones, preventing them from connecting easily.
2. **Solidifying:** It strengthens the F4 stone and prepares to build a formidable wall of influence along the F and G columns, which synergizes perfectly with the Tengen stone at G7.
3. **Territorial Pressure:** It keeps White's bottom stones low (on the 3rd or 2nd line), limiting their potential for central expansion and future territory.

Alternative moves like G3 or G4 are also strong, but F3 is more solid and ensures that Black's stones remain connected while continuing the strategy of dominating the center of the 13x13 board.

action.thoughts:
To determine the best move, we need to analyze the local tactical situation in the bottom left corner. 

The sequence so far in that area was: White played the 4-4 equivalent point at D4, Black approached high at F4, and White played a diagonal attachment underneath at E3. Black has just responded by playing the hane at F3. 

Black's hane at F3 leaves behind a severe defect in their shape at E4. 
If White ignores this area, Black will play E4 themselves, creating a perfect wall that seals White into the corner while generating immense outward thickness. 

To counter this and punish the aggressive hane, White must immediately wedge and cut at **E4**. 
Playing E4 achieves two critical goals simultaneously:
1. **Connection:** E4 is adjacent to both White's D4 and E3 stones. By playing there, White solidifies their L-shape structure, firmly connecting the two stones and eliminating any diagonal weaknesses.
2. **Severing Black's Shape:** E4 pushes right into the gap of Black's stones. By occupying this vital point, White cuts Black's F4 and F3 stones off from developing freely towards the left side, keeping Black split and short on liberties. 

Because White already has a supporting stone at H3, any ensuing fight resulting from this cut will be heavily in White's favor, as Black's stones on the bottom edge will struggle to find a comfortable base.

action.thoughts:
To determine the best move, we need to evaluate the local tactical situation on the bottom left, as well as the whole-board context.

**Local Analysis:**
The sequence of moves at the bottom left started with Black approaching the 4-4 point at F4, and White attaching at E4. Black haned at F3, and White cross-haned (or hane inside) at E3. Black then chose to extend to G4. 
This leaves us with a highly symmetrical contact shape:
- White has an L-shaped group on the left: D4, E4, E3.
- Black has an L-shaped group on the right: F4, G4, F3.

Black's extension to G4 leaves a critical weakness in White's shape. The intersection of **D3** is the vital point for both players here. 
If White neglects to play there, Black can strike at D3. A play by Black at D3 hits the inner angle of White's stones, robbing White of eye space, threatening to push through to the corner (C3/C4) or crosscut at E2, and essentially turning White's group into a heavy, floating target.

By playing at **D3** yourself, you form a solid "2x2 square" (D4, E4, D3, E3). While a 2x2 square in the center of the board is sometimes considered a slow "dumpling" shape, when it is attached to the side/corner like this, it is an incredibly thick and robust shape ("honte"). It perfectly eliminates all cutting aji (weaknesses), secures a massive corner base, and radiates power across the board. 

**Whole Board Strategy:**
White already has a stone at H3, which naturally caps and restricts Black's F3-F4-G4 group from expanding easily along the bottom right. Because Black's development on that side is already constrained by H3, White does not need to aggressively attack Black with a move like F2 (which would leave behind weaknesses). Simply taking the thick, profitable move at D3 gives White a clear advantage in secure territory and leaves Black over-concentrated.

"Play thick and you will never be sorry." Securing the vital point of the L-shape guarantees White an excellent game.

```json
{
  "move": "d3"
}
```

action.thoughts:

Black has just played at `m4`, extending their 4th-line wall (`j4`, `k4`, `l4`, `m4`) towards the right edge of the board. White currently has a parallel "finger" of stones right above it on the 5th and 6th lines (`j7`, `j6`, `k6`, `l6`, `l5`, `m5`). White's stones are solidly connected all the way back to the massive `h`-file wall, meaning they are completely safe and immune to being captured.

With Black pushing at `m4`, White needs to keep ahead in this crawling battle to prevent Black from turning the corner and expanding their bottom-right territory.

White has two main options here:
1. **Extend to `n5`:** This passively reaches the right edge (the `n` column is the 13th line, which is the edge of the board). However, this allows Black to play `n4`, completing their wall securely and making definitive points on the bottom right.
2. **Hane at `n4`:** By playing `n4`, White firmly blocks the head of Black's wall and stops their forward momentum. 

Let's evaluate if the hane at `n4` is safe.
If White plays `n4`, can Black cut by playing at `n5`? 
If Black plays `n5` (cross-cut), we must look at the liberties of that stone. Black's `n5` would be surrounded by White's `m5` on the left, White's `n4` on the bottom, and the edge of the board on the right. This means Black's `n5` would only have a single liberty remaining at `n6` (it is in self-atari). White can trivially capture the cutting stone by playing `n6`. 

Because Black cannot successfully cut at `n5`, White's hane at `n4` is 100% safe. Black will be forced to respond from below (e.g., at `n3` or `m3`), after which White can comfortably connect at `n5` or push elsewhere. By stepping down to `n4`, White exerts maximum pressure, restricts Black's bottom-right territory as much as possible, and maintains control of the endgame boundary.

**Evaluating the Best Move:**

action.thoughts:
To determine the best move, we need to evaluate the board state, focusing on the endgame boundaries since both players' groups are completely alive and solid.

**1. Analyze the Recent Moves:**
White's last move was at `m6`, which is a 2nd-line territorial push on the right side.
Black ignored this local push and played tenuki at `l10`, expanding his top-right framework. 

**2. Evaluate the Top Right (Black's `l10`):**
Black's `l10` expands his territory along the 10th row. However, because White has no nearby supporting stones in the top right (White's H-column wall is too far to connect an invasion), White cannot successfully invade or play a direct local block like `m10`. A solitary stone at `m10` or `k11` would be easily cut off and captured by Black. 

**3. Evaluate the Local Punishment (`m7`):**
Since Black tenukied White's `m6` push, the `m` column is still open. White can punish Black's tenuki by continuing to push at `m7`.
*   **If White plays `m7`:** This is a solid 2nd-line push and a hane against Black's `l7`. It threatens to march further into Black’s territory with `m8` and `m9`, effectively destroying the points Black just tried to build. Therefore, `m7` is **sente**; Black will be forced to respond and block (likely at `m8` or `n7`).
*   **If Black gets to play `m7`:** Black will bend around White's `m6`, significantly reducing White's bottom right corner points and locking in the boundary in gote.

**4. Check Other Board Areas:**
*   **Bottom Left (`e2`):** White could play `e2` to block Black's `f2` from sliding into the empty 2nd-line space (`d2`, `c2`). While large (a reverse-sente block), pushing at `m7` carries more immediate initiative (sente). Once Black answers the push, White might still get the chance to take `e2`.
*   **Top Left / Center (`g12` / `f13`):** White's `f12` and `g11` stones are safely connected to the `h11` wall (Black wedging at `g12` is self-atari because it is surrounded by `f12`, `h12`, and `g11`). Thus, no emergency defensive moves are required here. 

**Conclusion:**
In the endgame, keeping the initiative by playing sente pushes is critical. `m7` is a highly valuable, risk-free sente extension that punishes Black's tenuki and directly undermines Black's newly expanded top-right framework.

Black would get `e2`, force you to block at `d2`, and then follow up with another sente drop to `e1`, forcing you to answer at `d1`. - **c5**: A 3rd-line mutual boundary point. If you play here, you secure your 4th line and Black defends at `b5`. It's a swing of about 2 points.
- **n3**: A first-line hane at the right edge. You get `n3` and Black defends at `n2`, another swing of about 2 points.

`e2` is mathematically the most valuable boundary point left on the board due to its follow-up potential on the first line.

Black recently played **a7**, adding a stone to their large string at `b8-c8-d8-b7-c7-b6`.
Let's count the liberties of this 7-stone Black group:
1. **a8** (adjacent to `a7` and `b8`)
2. **a6** (adjacent to `a7` and `b6`)
3. **b5** (adjacent to `b6`)

Black is completely boxed in from the top and right by solid, living White groups (`b9-c9-d9-e9` and `c6-d6-e6-d7-e7-e8`). The group has zero internal eyes. To survive, Black must run towards the empty bottom-left corner (`a1-c5`).

- If White plays `a6`, White is playing into a space with only 1 liberty (`a5`), putting themselves in self-atari. Black would immediately respond with `a5`, capturing the stone and making an escape.
- If White plays `a8`, Black will push out with `b5`. By playing `b5`, Black extends into the wide-open bottom corner, multiplying their liberties (`c5, b4, a5`) and easily surviving or winning a capturing race.

The vital point to cleanly execute the kill is **b5**.

If White plays **b5**, White cleanly robs Black of their downward escape route. Let's trace the forced continuation:
1. White plays **b5**. The Black group is reduced to exactly 2 liberties: `a6` and `a8`.

4. Black plays **a8**, desperately extending to `a9`.
5. White plays **a9** (which safely connects to the strong `b9` White wall). Black's entire structure loses its final liberty and is cleanly captured.

Playing `n2` instead of `m1` would be a blunder because your `n2` stone would spawn with only 1 liberty (`n1`), allowing Black to capture it immediately and effectively wriggle out with more liberties.
